start.text: Good, you made it, {playerName}. The city map is not just about streets. It is about who can actually use them.

choices.label: Listen carefully.

map.text: Look here. The same city creates different routes for different people.

choices.label: Open the map later.

start.text: Accessibility is not a special request. It is how the city becomes public.

choices.label: Ask what barrier should be fixed first.

entrance.text: Start with the entrance. If people cannot enter, they cannot participate.

choices.label: Promise to check the entrance.

start.text: The Crossroads. Buses, stalls, shortcuts and queues: this is where the district decides who arrives, who waits and who gives up.

choices.label: Look around.

start.text: Main Bus Hub. Three lines by day, one after dinner. The timetable assumes nobody works evenings.

choices.label: Note the missing evening line.
Leave.

noted.text: You write it down. Evidence is how a complaint becomes a proposal.

choices.label: Done.

start.text: The market is the district's living room: half the stalls take cash only, and the vendors know everyone's name.

choices.label: Chat with a vendor.
Move on.

start.text: A narrow crossing between parked cars. Fine on foot, a puzzle with a stroller, impossible in a wheelchair. Ben would have measured it already.

choices.label: Take note for Ben.
Squeeze through.

start.text: Civic Info Point. Opening hours: shorter than the queue. Still, the noticeboard is honest work.

choices.label: Read the noticeboard.
Move on.

start.text: Three interviews, three districts, no solutions in your pocket. If you come back with a proposal instead of three stories, you've done the wrong mission.

choices.label: I'll go and listen. To the silences too.
I've already talked to people, I know what's needed.

resolution.text: (leafing through the empathy maps) Look: three neighbourhoods, three different ways of saying “nobody ever comes back”. That's where we start.

start.text: That crossing blocks children, older people and anyone on wheels — wheelchair or pram. The Council “will assess”; I've stopped waiting for verbs in the future tense.

choices.label: We do the temporary fix AND the real proposal.
I'll report the problem to the technical office and be done.

resolution.text: (testing the ramp) It holds. And the difference between today and a ticket is that tonight Lia crosses on her own.

start.text: I can give you a photo with the mayoral sash, or a mandate with delegated powers and a budget. The first is free; the second you have to make defensible in council.

choices.label: Here are names, dates and reviews.
Let's hold the event, the rest will sort itself out.

resolution.text: (signing) Know this: skip one review and the council will throw it in my face for years. (smiles) Don't skip it.

start.text: Thirty years ago that pier was one long party; now you want to “regenerate” it. Regenerate — conjugated from the verb to forget. (theatrical pause) Convince me otherwise.

choices.label: Tell me about it. We put it inside the project, not in place of the project.
The past is past, Donald.

resolution.text: (at the dinner, moved) All right, all right. If memory serves to open the future instead of barring it… then growing old has been of some use.

start.text: One Saturday, twenty hands, zero money. Courtyard 17 can become a common room — or stay the place where the Corporate hangs his rendering: pick the Saturday.

choices.label: I'll organise the crews and materials.
We'll call in a firm, sooner or later.

resolution.text: (wiping her hands) It's not finished — common things never are, you maintain them. That's the beauty of them.

start.text: I've got two drafts: “THE USUAL SUSPECTS' CENTER” — twelve thousand guaranteed clicks — or the true story, which is slower and less sexy. My editor wants the first by tonight; give me the second.

choices.label: Here are the accounts, the photos and three people to interview. Check everything.
Write whatever you want, I don't have time.

resolution.text: (showing the piece) “Participation isn't dead: it changed address.” Fewer clicks, more true. (sighs) I hate it when you people improve me.

start.text: A hundred rumours pass through here every day — eighty true, twenty poisonous — and I sort them by ear. Help me turn this into a proper information point, without switching off the chatter: the chatter is half the neighbourhood.

choices.label: A verified noticeboard, five languages, and your ear stays editor-in-chief.
A sign with the Council's opening hours will do.

resolution.text: (arranging the noticeboard) Look: notices in Urdu, Polish and Italian, and people's little slips underneath, same as ever. Order and warmth — like tea.

start.text: Everyone asks me to “turn a blind eye” — I have two, and a rulebook. But there is Article 9, collaboration pacts: nobody ever uses it because it takes work… fancy some?

choices.label: We write the pact properly, clause by clause.
We just open it, we'll see later.

resolution.text: (stamping) Collaboration Pact no. 1. (almost smiling) Did you know that rules, used well, are quite moving?

start.text: The last usable run out of Hill Gardens is 19:40; whoever works the evening shift walks home, forty minutes, in the dark. I've had the numbers in my head for years — nobody's ever asked me for them.

choices.label: I'm asking. Let's count a week of runs together.
Send the company a complaint.

resolution.text: (dossier on the dashboard) Twenty-three people on foot every night — now it's in writing. Let's see what weighs more at that table: my odometer or their Excel sheet.

start.text: I've done the sums: twelve active volunteers, four on the edge of burnout — if one collapses, everyone's shifts collapse. Caring for those who care isn't a luxury: it's maintenance of the network.

choices.label: Shifts with mandatory rest, pairs, and the right to say no.
They're adults, they can pace themselves.

resolution.text: (pinning up the rota) “Resting is participating.” Piotr suggested that line — he's back.

start.text: Look at my bar: the pensioners' table, the kids' table, the newcomers' table — three islands. Same walls, zero words between them; I've got the space, what I'm missing is the excuse.

choices.label: Theme nights and a shared noticeboard. We build the excuse together.
Put up a sign saying 'talk to each other'.

resolution.text: (gesturing at the room) Thursday: backgammon tournament, Donald versus the Youth Court kids. He lost and wants a rematch. (under his breath) When false rumours go round now, they fall apart on their own in here: people know each other.

start.text: Pots in the windows, a painted bench, an adopted flowerbed — small, yes. But a tended place says “someone is staying here”; what matters is that it stays in the residents' hands and doesn't become a shop window for tourists.

choices.label: Minimal green, co-tended, with the names of those who look after it.
We'll call a garden centre and be done.

resolution.text: (watering with a little girl) There are three rules: who plants, tends; who tends, signs; who signs, tells the story. Beauty without keepers is only scenery.

start.text: You adults measure streets in metres; I measure them in “where I can go on my own”. Want to see my map? It's full of holes.

choices.label: You lead. I'll write down everything you say.
Children don't decide these things.

resolution.text: (at the noticeboard, showing the pinned-up map) That red X — I put it there. And they copied it into the real document. (to Zoe) See? Sometimes they do listen to us.

start.text: Everyone talks about the climate in the future tense; I have Lake Edge's drains full of leaves now, and a list of low houses that go under after two hours of rain. Prevention is boring — that's why nobody does it.

choices.label: A channel-clearing day + evacuation list + assembly point. Boring stuff — let's do it.
If it happens, we'll call civil protection.

resolution.text: (watching the sky) Now we're even allowed to hope it won't be needed. Hoping AFTER you've cleared the channels is a different kind of hope altogether.

start.text: I taught for forty years; now I watch intelligent adults sit silent in meetings for fear of a grammar mistake. Language is the city's first door — I still have the key, if anyone is interested.

choices.label: A language and citizenship course. And your former pupils as tutors.
There are apps for learning languages.

resolution.text: (after the first lesson) Mrs Anh asked a question in public today. Wrong subjunctive, perfect courage. (adjusting her glasses) We shall correct the subjunctive.

start.text: The papers only ever cover Hill Gardens in the crime pages; I can draw, and the kids at the wall know what needs drawing — it's called counter-narrative. But if I'm the only one signing it, that's just another theft.

choices.label: Co-creation: the residents choose the stories, you draw them, you sign together.
You do it, you're the artist.

resolution.text: (in front of the mural signed by 14 hands) My name is the smallest one. I've never been this happy about a bad placement.

start.text: I deliver even where the doorbells have lost their names. Mr Ferro, third floor — I'm the only voice he hears all week, and I know twenty more like him: no list holds them.

choices.label: Mostrami chi è isolato.
Più tardi.

choose.text: Eccoli: anziani soli, nuovi arrivati. Cosa facciamo?

choices.label: Colleghiamoli a una rete di cura.
Solo un censimento, per ora.

resolution.text: (nearly weeping) Mr Ferro made coffee for two neighbours. Twenty-three years without opening his door to anyone — twenty-three! (blows his nose) Excuse me, it's the dust.

start.text: (flat voice, contract already printed) Full refurbishment, ninety days, twenty-year management to us — the neighbourhood gets a beautiful space, and the use, well… gets regulated. Sign here.

choices.label: Sign the contract. Ninety days, a beautiful space.
Countersign the civic pact: guaranteed public hours, social tariffs, an annual review.
We already have ninety neighbours. We don't need ninety days.

resolution.text: (closing the briefcase, without rancour) Interesting. Tired cities usually sign. (at the door) We'll be back when you're tired.
